bill.short_title: Fire Grants and Safety Act of 2023

provisions.statutory_reference: Section 17(g)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2216(g)(1))
provisions.raw_text: Section 17(g)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2216(g)(1)) is amended—(1) in subparagraph (L), by striking ''and''
notes: Authorization of appropriations (discretionary), not mandatory spending. Adds new subparagraph (N) authorizing $95,000,000/year for FY2024-FY2028.
Also corrects a drafting error ('for for') in existing subparagraph (M).

cross_references.target: Section 17(g)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2216(g)(1))
cross_references.description: Adds new subparagraph (N)

provisions.program_name: United States Fire Administration — Section 8(f) activities
provisions.statutory_reference: Section 17(g)(1)(N) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (as added by this section)
provisions.raw_text: of which $3,420,000 for each such fiscal year shall be used to carry out section 8(f).
notes: Sub-allocation of the $95,000,000 annual authorization. $3,420,000 per year earmarked for section 8(f) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act.

cross_references.target: Section 8(f) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974
cross_references.description: Activities funded by this sub-allocation

provisions.description: Extends the sunset date for the Assistance to Firefighters Grants Program and Fire Prevention and Safety Grants Program from 2024 to 2030.
provisions.policy_area: Fire safety grants
provisions.raw_text: Section 33(r) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229(r)) is amended by striking ''2024'' and inserting ''2030''.
notes: Subsection (a) — Sunset extension. Extends program authority by 6 years.

cross_references.target: Section 33(r) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229(r))
cross_references.description: Extends sunset from 2024 to 2030

provisions.program_name: Assistance to Firefighters Grants Program and Fire Prevention and Safety Grants Program
provisions.statutory_reference: Section 33(q)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229(q)(1))
provisions.raw_text: Section 33(q)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229(q)(1)) is amended by striking ''to carry out this section—'' an
notes: Subsection (b) — Authorization of appropriations (discretionary). Replaces prior tiered authorization structure with flat $750,000,000/year for FY2024-FY2028.

cross_references.target: Section 33(q)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229(q)(1))

provisions.description: Extends the sunset date for the Staffing for Adequate Fire and Emergency Response (SAFER) Grant Program from 2024 to 2030.
provisions.policy_area: Fire safety grants
provisions.raw_text: Section 34(k) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229a(k)) is amended by striking ''2024'' and inserting ''2030''.
notes: Subsection (a) — Sunset extension. Extends SAFER program authority by 6 years.

cross_references.target: Section 34(k) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229a(k))
cross_references.description: Extends sunset from 2024 to 2030

provisions.program_name: Staffing for Adequate Fire and Emergency Response Grant Program

provisions.raw_text: Section 34(j)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229a(j)(1)(I)) is amended—(1) in subparagraph (G), by inserting ''
notes: Subsection (b) — Authorization of appropriations (discretionary). Modifies subparagraph (H) to extend authorization to FY2024-FY2028 and strikes subparagraph (I). The dollar amount is not explicitly stated in this amendment text; it is the existing level in subparagraph (H) of current law, which is carried forward to the new fiscal years.

cross_references.target: Section 34(j)(1) of the Federal Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 (15 U.S.C. 2229a(j)(1))
cross_references.description: Extends existing authorization level to FY2024-FY2028, strikes subparagraph (I)

provisions.description: Requires the Comptroller General to conduct an audit of and issue a publicly available report on barriers preventing fire departments from accessing Federal funds and on the United States Fire Administration.
deadlines: not later than three years after the date of the enactment of this Act
provisions.raw_text: Not later than three years after the date of the enactment of this Act, the Comptroller General of the United States shall conduct an audit of and issue
notes: GAO audit covers two topics: (1) barriers that prevent fire departments from accessing Federal funds, and (2) the United States Fire Administration.

provisions.description: Directs the NRC to coordinate all work relating to import/export licensing and international regulatory cooperation for nuclear reactors and radioactive materials; support interagency and international coordination on technical standards, capacity building in foreign countries, and exchange programs/training; authorizes establishment of International Nuclear Export and Innovation Branch within Office of International Programs; requires interagency coordination with Secretaries of State and Energy; includes savings clause preserving existing NRC authority.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy international cooperation and export regulation
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 101. 
International nuclear export and innovation activities
(a) 
Commission coordination
(1) 
In general
The Commission shall—(A) coordinate all work of the Commission relating to—
notes: Subsection (a) establishes NRC coordination duties for international nuclear export and innovation activities
Subsection (b) authorizes (but does not require) establishment of 'International Nuclear Export and Innovation Branch' within the Office of International Programs
Subsection (d) requires coordination with Secretary of State, Secretary of Energy, and other agencies
Subsection (e) is a savings clause preserving existing NRC licensing and regulatory authority
No dollar amounts or appropriations in this provision

provisions.description: Amends Section 102 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215) to exclude costs for international nuclear export and innovation activities from the NRC fee base, effective October 1, 2025.
provisions.policy_area: NRC fee structure for international nuclear activities
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
(c) 
Exclusion of international activities from the fee base
(1) 
In general
Section 102 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215) is amended—
notes: Subsection (c) of SEC. 101; extracted separately due to statutory amendment
Adds new paragraph (4) to subsection (a) requiring identification of international nuclear export activities in budget justification
Adds new clause (iv) to subsection (b)(1)(B) excluding costs for these activities from fee base
Effective date: October 1, 2025 (beginning of FY2026)

cross_references.target: Section 102 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215)
cross_references.description: Adds exclusion for international nuclear export and innovation activity costs from NRC fee base

provisions.description: Prohibits any person subject to NRC jurisdiction from possessing or owning covered fuel (enriched uranium fabricated outside the US into fuel assemblies by Russian or Chinese government-owned/controlled entities) without a specific NRC license. Establishes a consultation and joint determination process with Secretary of Energy and Secretary of State before license issuance, under which a license shall not be issued if they determine possession poses a national security threat or if no determination is made within 180 days.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear fuel supply chain national security
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 102. 
Denial of certain domestic licenses for national security purposes
(a) 
Definition of covered fuel
In this section, the term covered fuel means enriched uranium that is fabricated outside
notes: Covered fuel is enriched uranium fabricated outside the US into fuel assemblies by entities owned/controlled by or subject to jurisdiction of Russia or China
NRC must notify Secretaries of Energy and State within 30 days of receiving license application
Secretaries have 180 days to make joint determination on national security threat
Commission must notify Congress within 30 days of determination and make public within 15 days after that
If no determination is made, license shall not be issued (subsection (c)(2)(C))
Savings clause preserves existing treaties and international agreements

cross_references.target: Section 53 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2073)
cross_references.description: License to possess or own covered fuel issued under this section and part 70 of title 10, CFR

provisions.description: Requires the NRC to notify appropriate committees of Congress (including Senate Foreign Relations, Senate Energy and Natural Resources, and House Foreign Affairs) when it issues an export license for specified nuclear items to countries that have not ratified an IAEA Additional Protocol or the amendment to the Convention on the Physical Protection of Nuclear Material.
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 103. 
Export license notification
(a) 
Definition of low-Enriched uranium
In this section, the term low-enriched uranium means uranium enriched to less than 20 percent of the uranium-235 isotope.
notes: Applicable items include: unirradiated nuclear fuel containing special nuclear material (excluding LEU), reactors using such fuel, and reprocessing/separation plant components listed in Appendix I to 10 CFR part 110
Low-enriched uranium (below 20% U-235) is excluded from the items triggering notification
Notification triggered for countries lacking IAEA Additional Protocol ratification or CPPNM amendment ratification
Notification is to appropriate committees of Congress plus specified foreign affairs and energy committees

cross_references.target: Section 11 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2014)
cross_references.description: Definition of special nuclear material

provisions.description: Requires the Secretary of Energy, in consultation with the Secretary of State, Secretary of Commerce, EPA Administrator, and NRC, to conduct a comprehensive study on the global status of the civilian nuclear energy industry and its supply chains within 1 year of enactment, and to submit the study to Congress within 180 days of completion.
deadlines: 1 year after enactment for study completion
180 days after study completion for congressional report
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 104. 
Global nuclear energy assessment
(a) 
Study required
Not later than 1 year after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary of Energy, in consultation with the Secretary of State,
notes: Study covers 10 major areas including: industry status and risks, emissions reduction potential, foreign policy role, allied country collaboration, competitor country nuclear diplomacy, nonproliferation impacts, domestic supply chain challenges, and policy recommendations
Report to Congress may include a classified annex
Recommendations include developing national strategy for nuclear diplomacy and modernizing regulatory requirements

provisions.description: Requires the Secretary of Energy, with concurrence of the Secretary of State, to identify and evaluate factors for determining a country's generally authorized destination status under 10 CFR part 810 within 90 days of enactment; update the DOE process for such determinations; and review and amend the Appendix A list within 1 year of enactment and at least once every 5 years thereafter.
deadlines: 90 days after enactment for identification and evaluation of factors
1 year after enactment for initial review and amendment of Appendix A list
At least once every 5 years thereafter for subsequent reviews
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 105. 
Process for review and amendment of part 810 generally authorized destinations
(a) 
Identification and Evaluation of Factors
Not later than 90 days after the date of enactment
notes: Factors to be identified are separate from (i.e., in addition to) agreements for cooperation under Section 123 of the Atomic Energy Act
Addresses Appendix A to 10 CFR part 810, which lists countries authorized for certain nuclear technology transfers
Establishes recurring 5-year review cycle for the generally authorized destinations list

cross_references.target: Section 123 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2153)
cross_references.description: Agreements for cooperation referenced as distinct from factors to be evaluated

provisions.description: Amends the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (P.L. 115-439) to add definitions for advanced nuclear reactor applicant, pre-applicant, agency support, hourly rate for mission-direct program salaries and benefits, mission-direct program salaries and benefits, and mission-indirect program support; caps the hourly rate charged to advanced nuclear reactor applicants and pre-applicants at the hourly rate for mission-direct program salaries and benefits (excluding mission-indirect program support and agency support costs); adds excluded activities for fee calculations; includes a sunset for pre-applicant fee provisions on September 30, 2030; and sets an effective date of October 1, 2025.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy regulation / NRC fee reform
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 201. 
Fees for advanced nuclear reactor application review
(a) 
Definitions
Section 3 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215 note; Public Law 115–439) is amended
notes: Subsection (a) adds seven new defined terms to Section 3 of NEIMA
Subsection (b) excludes mission-indirect and agency support costs from fees charged to advanced reactor applicants and pre-applicants
Subsection (c) replaces NEIMA Section 102(b)(2) with new fee-for-service provisions capping hourly rates for advanced reactor applicants and pre-applicants
Subsection (d) sunsets the pre-applicant fee provisions (paragraphs (1)(B)(vi) and (2)(C)) on September 30, 2030
Subsection (e) sets an effective date of October 1, 2025
No appropriation or spending provision; purely a policy/regulatory amendment

cross_references.target: Section 3 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (P.L. 115-439)
cross_references.description: Adds definitions

cross_references.target: Section 102(b)(1)(B) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215(b)(1)(B))
cross_references.description: Adds excluded activities for advanced reactor fee calculations

cross_references.target: Section 102(b)(2) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215(b))
cross_references.description: Replaces fee-for-service paragraph with provisions capping advanced reactor fee rates

cross_references.target: Section 102 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215)
cross_references.description: Adds sunset provision for pre-applicant fee provisions

provisions.description: Amends Section 103 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act to authorize the Secretary of Energy to award prizes to the first eligible entities (non-Federal entities and TVA) to receive an NRC operating license or combined license finding for advanced nuclear reactors, in amounts equal to total NRC fees collected from the awardee, across five award categories: (i) first advanced reactor licensed, (ii) first using spent fuel or depleted uranium fuel, (iii) first nuclear integrated energy system, (iv) first flexible-operation reactor for nonelectric applications, and (v) first licensed under the technology-inclusive regulatory framework. Subject to availability of appropriations. Federal funding limitations apply, excluding TVA power program funds from the definition of Federal funds.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy / Advanced reactor prize authority
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 202. 
Advanced nuclear reactor prizes
Section 103 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (Public Law 115–439; 132 Stat. 5571) is amended by adding at the end the following
notes: Authorizes prizes but does not appropriate funds — 'subject to the availability of appropriations'
Prize amount equals total NRC fees assessed and collected from the winning entity under Section 102(b)(2)
Five award categories covering different advanced reactor types and licensing pathways
Notwithstanding section 169 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2209)
TVA power program funds excluded from definition of Federal funds for cost-share calculations
Awards cannot exceed non-Federal expenditures (minus Federal funds and minimum cost-share under 42 U.S.C. 16352)
No repayment or dividend obligations for award recipients

cross_references.target: Section 103 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (P.L. 115-439; 132 Stat. 5571)
cross_references.description: Adds new subsection (f) on prizes for advanced nuclear reactor licensing

cross_references.target: Section 169 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2209)
cross_references.description: Prize authority notwithstanding NRC fee recovery requirements

cross_references.target: Section 988 of the Energy Policy Act of 2005 (42 U.S.C. 16352)
cross_references.description: Minimum cost-share requirement referenced in Federal funding limitation
Award recipients not required to repay or pay dividends

provisions.description: Requires the Commission (NRC) to submit a report to Congress within 270 days of enactment addressing unique licensing issues or requirements relating to: (1) flexible operation of advanced nuclear reactors, (2) use of advanced nuclear reactors exclusively for nonelectric applications, and (3) colocation of nuclear reactors with industrial or other facilities. Report must include stakeholder input and cover hydrogen production, water desalination, industrial process heat, district heating, energy storage, isotope production, and other applications, as well as cost estimates, proposed budgets, and timeframes for implementing regulatory guidance.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 203. 
Licensing considerations relating to use of nuclear energy for nonelectric applications
(a) 
In general
Not later than 270 days after the date of enactment of this Act, the Commission shall submit
notes: Report must address flexible power ramping, nonelectric-only operation, and colocation with industrial plants
Stakeholder input required from DOE, nuclear industry, technology developers, industrial/chemical/medical sectors, NGOs, and public
Report must describe options within existing regulatory framework, technology-inclusive framework under NEIMA Sec. 103(a)(4), or new rulemaking
Must include cost estimates, proposed budgets, and timeframes for implementing risk-informed and performance-based regulatory guidance

cross_references.target: Section 103(a)(4) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2133 note; P.L. 115-439)
cross_references.description: Technology-inclusive regulatory framework referenced as option for addressing licensing issues

provisions.description: Amends Section 102(b)(1)(B) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act to add NRC costs for review of early site permits to demonstrate advanced nuclear reactors on DOE sites or critical national security infrastructure sites, and related pre-application activities, to the list of costs excluded from NRC fee recovery.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy regulation
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 204. 
Enabling preparations for the demonstration of advanced nuclear reactors on Department of Energy sites or critical national security infrastructure
notes: Amends 42 U.S.C. 2215(b)(1)(B) by adding clause (vii) covering early site permit review costs and pre-application activities for advanced reactors on DOE or critical national security infrastructure sites
Effective date: October 1, 2025
No dollar amount specified; modifies NRC fee structure

cross_references.target: Section 102(b)(1)(B) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215(b)(1)(B))
cross_references.description: Adds clause (vii) covering early site permit costs for advanced reactors on DOE sites

cross_references.target: Section 327(d) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019 (Public Law 115-232)
cross_references.description: Definition of critical national security infrastructure

provisions.description: Amends the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 to define 'fusion machine' and modify the definition of byproduct material to account for fusion machines; redesignates and reorders several subsections of section 11 of the Atomic Energy Act.
provisions.policy_area: Fusion energy regulation
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 205. 
Fusion energy regulation
(a) 
Definition
Section 11 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2014) is amended—(1) in subsection e.—(A) i
notes: Defines 'fusion machine' as a machine capable of transforming atomic nuclei through fusion processes and directly capturing resultant products
Modifies byproduct material definition to include material made radioactive by a fusion machine (via particle accelerator clause)
Redesignates multiple subsections of Section 11 for alphabetical ordering

cross_references.target: Section 11 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2014)
cross_references.description: Adds definition of fusion machine and modifies byproduct material definition

provisions.description: Makes technical and conforming changes to the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act to incorporate the new 'fusion machine' definition, including adding fusion machine license applications to commercial advanced nuclear reactor license application references.
provisions.policy_area: Fusion energy regulation
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(b) 
Technical and conforming changes
(1) 
In general
Section 103(a) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2133 note
notes: Amends Section 103(a) of NEIMA to include fusion machine license applications
Amends Section 3 of NEIMA to replace 'fusion reactor' with 'reactor or fusion machine' and add definition of fusion machine

cross_references.target: Section 103(a) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2133 note; Public Law 115-439)
cross_references.description: Includes fusion machine license applications in commercial advanced nuclear reactor license applications

cross_references.target: Section 3 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2215 note; Public Law 115-439)
cross_references.description: Updates definitions to include fusion machine

provisions.description: Requires the NRC to submit a report to Congress within 1 year on risk- and performance-based, design-specific licensing frameworks for mass-manufactured fusion machines, including evaluation of the FAA certification model as a potential regulatory model, and an estimated timeline for issuing consolidated guidance or regulations.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(2) 
Requirement
Not later than 1 year after the date of enactment of this Act, the Commission shall submit to the appropriate committees of Congress a report
notes: Study must be conducted in consultation with Agreement States and private fusion sector
Must evaluate FAA aircraft certification process as potential model for mass-manufactured fusion machine regulations
Must include estimated timeline for issuing consolidated guidance or regulations

provisions.description: Requires the NRC to evaluate within 1 year the extent to which modification of regulations, guidance, or policy is needed to enable efficient, timely, and predictable licensing reviews for production or utilization facilities at brownfield sites and retired fossil fuel sites, considering reuse of existing infrastructure, early site permits, plant parameter envelopes, and standardized applications.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 206. 
Regulatory issues for nuclear facilities at brownfield sites
(a) 
Definitions
In this section:(1) 
Brownfield site
The term brownfield sit
notes: Defines covered sites as brownfield sites, retired fossil fuel sites, or both
Retired fossil fuel sites include partially shut down multi-unit facilities
Must consider reuse of switchyard, heat-sink, steam cycle components, roads, railroad access, and water availability

cross_references.target: Section 101 of the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act of 1980 (42 U.S.C. 9601)
cross_references.description: Definition of brownfield site

provisions.description: Requires the NRC to submit to Congress within 14 months a report describing any regulations, guidance, and policies identified in the evaluation of regulatory issues for nuclear facilities at brownfield and retired fossil fuel sites.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(3) 
Report
Not later than 14 months after the date of enactment of this Act, the Commission shall submit to the appropriate committees of Congress a re
notes: Report on regulations, guidance, and policies identified in SEC. 206(b)(1) evaluation

provisions.description: Requires the NRC within 2 years to either develop and implement strategies or initiate a rulemaking to enable efficient, timely, and predictable licensing reviews for production or utilization facilities at covered sites (brownfield and retired fossil fuel sites), considering existing infrastructure, emergency preparedness, historical environmental data, prior NEPA reviews, decommissioning activities, and community engagement.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(c) 
Licensing
(1) 
In general
Not later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this Act, the Commission shall—(A) develop and implement strategie
notes: Commission has choice between developing/implementing strategies or initiating a rulemaking
Must consider NEPA reviews, decommissioning, community engagement, and historical experience

cross_references.target: National Environmental Policy Act of 1969 (42 U.S.C. 4321 et seq.)
cross_references.description: Previously completed environmental reviews to be considered

provisions.description: Requires the NRC to submit to Congress within 3 years a report describing the actions taken to develop licensing strategies or initiate rulemaking for nuclear facilities at brownfield and retired fossil fuel sites.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(d) 
Report
Not later than 3 years after the date of enactment of this Act, the Commission shall submit to the appropriate committees of Congress a repor
notes: Report on actions taken under SEC. 206(c)(1)

provisions.description: Directs the NRC to establish and carry out an expedited procedure for issuing combined licenses for new nuclear reactors that reference certified designs or designs substantially similar to previously licensed reactors, proposed for sites where licensed commercial nuclear reactors operate or previously operated (or directly adjacent similar sites). Sets qualification criteria including no pending license suspension or revocation orders.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy licensing
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 207. 
Combined license review procedure
(a) 
In general
In accordance with this section, the Commission shall establish and carry out an expedited
notes: Applicant must reference a certified design or a design substantially similar to a previously licensed reactor
Site must be on or adjacent to an existing or former licensed commercial nuclear reactor site
Applicant cannot be subject to license suspension/revocation order under 10 CFR 2.202
Establishes strict timelines: 18 months for technical review and environmental review, 2 years for hearings, 25 months for final decision

cross_references.target: Section 185 b. of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2235(b))
cross_references.description: Combined license authority under which expedited procedure is established

cross_references.target: National Environmental Policy Act of 1969 (42 U.S.C. 4321 et seq.)
cross_references.description: Environmental review requirements applicable to expedited procedure

provisions.description: Requires the NRC Executive Director for Operations to inform the Commission within 30 days of any failure to meet the expedited combined license review deadlines (18 months for technical review, 2 years for hearings, 25 months for final decision). If any deadline is exceeded by 90 days, the Commission must submit to Congress a report with a detailed explanation and completion plan.
deadlines: 90 days after missed deadline triggers congressional report
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(d) 
Performance and reporting
(1) 
Delays in issuance
Not later than 30 days after the applicable deadline, the Executive Director for Operations of th
notes: Two-tier reporting: internal notification to Commission at 30 days, congressional report at 90 days
Congressional report must include detailed explanation and completion plan

provisions.description: Requires the NRC within 18 months to develop risk-informed and performance-based strategies and guidance to license and regulate micro-reactors, covering staffing, operations, oversight, inspections, safeguards, security, emergency preparedness, risk analysis methods, decommissioning funding assurance, transportation of fueled micro-reactors, and siting (including population density, mobile deployment, and environmental reviews). Requires implementation within 3 years through existing framework, technology-inclusive framework, or rulemaking.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 208. 
Regulatory requirements for micro-reactors
(a) 
Micro-Reactor licensing
The Commission shall—(1) not later than 18 months after the date of
notes: Covers comprehensive licensing topics: staffing, oversight, safeguards, security, emergency preparedness, risk analysis, decommissioning, transportation, and siting
Must consider population density criteria from SECY-20-0045 and policy issues from SECY-20-0093
Implementation may be through existing framework, NEIMA section 103(a)(4) technology-inclusive framework, or new rulemaking
Must consider unique micro-reactor characteristics: physical size, design simplicity, source term
Must consult with Secretary of Energy, other federal agencies, micro-reactor developers, and other stakeholders

cross_references.target: Section 103 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2133)
cross_references.description: Licensing authority for commercial utilization facilities

cross_references.target: Section 103(a)(4) of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (42 U.S.C. 2133 note; Public Law 115-439)
cross_references.description: Technology-inclusive regulatory framework as potential implementation pathway
NRC policy paper on population-related siting considerations for advanced reactors (May 8, 2020)
NRC policy paper on policy and licensing considerations related to micro-reactors (October 6, 2020)

provisions.description: Creates an exception to the prohibition on issuing nuclear utilization facility licenses to foreign-owned, -controlled, or -dominated entities under sections 103(d) and 104(d) of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, allowing such licenses for entities owned, controlled, or dominated by OECD member countries (excluding sanctioned countries), the Republic of India, corporations incorporated in those countries, or citizens/nationals of those countries, provided the Commission determines issuance is not inimical to the common defense and security or the health and safety of the public.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy / foreign ownership of nuclear facilities
provisions.title: III
provisions.raw_text: The prohibitions against issuing certain licenses for utilization facilities to certain aliens, corporations, and other entities described in the second
notes: Subsection (a) establishes the exception; subsection (b) defines eligible entities (OECD members except sanctioned countries, India, corporations/citizens thereof)
Paragraph (b)(2) excludes countries subject to sanctions under section 231 of CAATSA (22 U.S.C. 9525) or whose nationals are on the SDN list under those sanctions
No dollar amounts in this provision

cross_references.target: Section 103 d. of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2133(d))
cross_references.description: Creates exception to foreign ownership prohibition for nuclear facility licenses

cross_references.target: Section 104 d. of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2134(d))
cross_references.description: Creates exception to foreign ownership prohibition for nuclear facility licenses

cross_references.target: Section 231 of the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (22 U.S.C. 9525)
cross_references.description: Basis for country exclusion from eligibility

provisions.description: Technical amendment to Section 103 d. of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 correcting a typographical error by striking 'any any' and inserting 'any'.
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy / technical correction
provisions.title: III
provisions.raw_text: Section 103 d. of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2133(d)) is amended, in the second sentence, by striking ''any any'' and inserting ''any''.
notes: Subsection (c) of SEC. 301
Corrects a duplicated word in existing statute

cross_references.description: Technical correction striking duplicated 'any'

provisions.description: Savings clause providing that nothing in section 301 affects the requirements of section 721 of the Defense Production Act of 1950 (CFIUS review authority).
provisions.policy_area: Nuclear energy / national security review
provisions.title: III
provisions.raw_text: Nothing in this section affects the requirements of section 721 of the Defense Production Act of 1950 (50 U.S.C. 4565).
notes: Subsection (d) of SEC. 301
Preserves CFIUS review authority over foreign investment in nuclear facilities even when the foreign ownership licensing prohibition is waived

provisions.description: Requires the Commission (NRC) to submit to appropriate committees of Congress a report on manufacturing and construction for nuclear energy projects within 180 days of enactment, examining advanced manufacturing processes, advanced construction techniques, nuclear-grade component requirements, licensing issues, and identifying options within existing regulatory frameworks or through new rulemaking. Report must include cost estimates, proposed budgets, and timeframes for implementing risk-informed and performance-based regulatory guidance.

provisions.description: Requires the Commission (NRC) to establish an initiative to enhance preparedness and coordination with respect to the qualification and licensing of advanced nuclear fuel. Within 180 days, the Commission and the Secretary of Energy shall enter into a memorandum of understanding to share technical expertise, enable testing and demonstration of accident tolerant fuels, operate a shared database, and ensure sufficient technical expertise and facility access for advanced nuclear fuel development and regulatory review.

notes: No dollar amounts in this section

Subsection (b) requires MOU between NRC and DOE within 180 days covering technical expertise sharing, database, facility access, and modeling/simulation consultation
MOU covers accident tolerant fuels, advanced nuclear reactor fuel concepts, and private-sector testing/demonstration

provisions.description: Requires the Commission (NRC) to submit to appropriate committees of Congress, within 2 years of enactment, a report describing efforts under the advanced nuclear fuel initiative, including an assessment of preparedness to review and qualify accident tolerant fuel, ceramic cladding materials, fuels containing silicon carbide, HALEU fuels, molten-salt based liquid fuels, fuels derived from spent nuclear fuel or depleted uranium, and other related fuel concepts. Report must also describe MOU activities, areas of research needed, and other challenges.

Covers seven categories of advanced nuclear fuel types

provisions.description: Requires the Commission to update its mission statement within 1 year to include that licensing and regulation of civilian use of radioactive materials and nuclear energy be conducted efficiently and not unnecessarily limit civilian use or its benefits to society, consistent with existing Atomic Energy Act and Energy Reorganization Act policies. On completion, requires a report to Congress describing the updated mission statement and guidance to staff.
deadlines: upon completion of mission statement update

provisions.description: Amends the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 by inserting a new Section 161B establishing NRC Commission workforce authorities including: (1) direct hire authority for up to 210 exceptionally well-qualified individuals into excepted service covered positions plus up to 20 term-limited positions (up to 4-year terms) per fiscal year; (2) authority to fix compensation for covered positions without regard to GS classification, capped at Executive Schedule Level III; (3) one-time hiring bonuses up to $25,000 and performance bonuses up to $25,000 (with 5-year cooldown); (4) implementation plan due within 180 days; (5) delegation to Executive Director for Operations; (6) annual reporting on hiring, vacancies, and compensation starting FY2026; and (7) sunset on September 30, 2034, extendable to September 30, 2039 by certification.
provisions.policy_area: NRC workforce and hiring authority
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text:
The Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2011 et seq.) is amended by inserting after section 161A the following:
SEC. 161B. 
Commission workforce
notes: Creates new Section 161B of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954
Direct hire authority: up to 210 individuals under subsection (a)(1)(A); up to 20 term-limited per fiscal year under (a)(1)(B)
Compensation cap at Executive Schedule Level III; excludes Senior Executive Service positions
Hiring bonus up to $25,000; performance bonus up to $25,000 with 5-year cooldown between performance bonuses
Exceptional performance includes timely licensing reviews, resolution of novel regulatory issues, process improvements
Sunset: September 30, 2034, extendable to September 30, 2039 upon Commission certification
Termination does not affect compensation already fixed under these authorities
Also amends table of contents of the Atomic Energy Act

cross_references.target: Atomic Energy Act of 1954 (42 U.S.C. 2011 et seq.)
cross_references.description: Inserts new Section 161B after Section 161A

provisions.description: Requires the Commission to submit to Congress within 180 days a report on efforts to facilitate efficient, timely, and predictable environmental reviews of nuclear reactor license applications, including through expanded use of categorical exclusions, environmental assessments, and generic environmental impact statements. The report must address implementation of NEPA amendments from the Fiscal Responsibility Act of 2023, consider numerous streamlining measures, and include a rulemaking schedule.

provisions.description: Requires the Commission to develop and submit to Congress within 1 year a report identifying specific improvements to the nuclear reactor and materials oversight and inspection programs to maximize efficiency through risk-informed, performance-based procedures, information technologies, and staff training. The report must seek stakeholder input and address specific areas including travel resources, inspection planning, document handling, duplicative activities, training, AI/ML, and licensee innovation.
deadlines: 1 year after the date of enactment

Improving oversight and inspection programs
(a) 
Definition of licensee
In this section, the term licensee means a person that holds a license issued under section 103 or 104 of the Ato
notes: Requires stakeholder input from other federal agencies, nuclear energy industry, NGOs, and public.

Report must assess 7 major areas (subsection (d) paragraphs (1)-(7)) including oversight modifications via technology, measures to eliminate duplication, risk-informed procedures, licensee innovations, and implementation timeline.

provisions.description: Amends Section 104 c. of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 to restructure provisions on research and development activities, adding a limitation on utilization facilities specifying that not more than 75% of annual costs may be devoted to sale of nonenergy services, energy, or a combination thereof (other than for R&D or education/training), and not more than 50% to the sale of energy.

flagged_issues: This is an authorization bill, not an appropriations bill. All dollar amounts are authorization ceilings for discretionary programs, not actual budget authority.
SEC. 4(b) extends the SAFER authorization to FY2024-2028 but the specific dollar amount is not stated in the amendment text — it is carried forward from existing law.
SEC. 2 contains a typo in the bill text: 'subparagraraph' instead of 'subparagraph'.
This is an authorization bill (ADVANCE Act of 2024), not an appropriations bill — contains no dollar-amount appropriations
SEC. 101(c) amends NEIMA Section 102 to exclude international activities from NRC fee base, effective October 1, 2025
SEC. 102 creates new licensing restrictions on Russian- and Chinese-origin enriched uranium fuel assemblies
SEC. 202 authorizes prizes subject to availability of appropriations but does not itself appropriate funds
Title II contains no direct appropriations — all provisions are policy amendments or reporting requirements
SEC. 201 amends NRC fee structure effective October 1, 2025, with pre-applicant provisions sunsetting September 30, 2030
Title II contains no appropriations — all provisions are policy riders and regulatory directives amending nuclear energy law
SEC. 204 amends NEIMA as previously amended by section 201(b), indicating dependency on earlier section in this bill
SEC. 205 amends definitions previously amended by section 201(a), indicating dependency on earlier section in this bill
Title III contains only SEC. 301 with no appropriations — entirely policy provisions modifying the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 to ease foreign ownership restrictions on nuclear facilities
Title IV contains no appropriations or dollar amounts — all provisions are directives or policy amendments
SEC. 402 amends existing law (42 U.S.C. 16274a) to create a traineeship subprogram but does not authorize specific funding levels
SEC. 404 has two distinct directive components extracted separately: the MOU requirement (180 days) and the report requirement (2 years)
Title V contains no appropriations — it is entirely policy/authorization provisions relating to NRC mission and workforce
SEC. 502(a) creates new Section 161B of the Atomic Energy Act with significant NRC hiring and compensation authorities sunsetting September 30, 2034 (extendable to 2039)
Title V contains no dollar-amount provisions; all sections are policy/authorization riders or reporting directives amending existing nuclear regulatory statutes.
SEC. 503(b) sets a 30% cap on NRC corporate support costs starting FY2025 by amending NEIMA.
SEC. 503(c) narrows the definition of corporate support costs to exclude Three White Flint North unoccupied space and Office of the Commission costs.
SEC. 504 shortens a performance metric deadline from 180 to 90 days and adds triennial review requirement.
SEC. 505 contains three subsections each amending different statutes to improve licensing efficiency.
